title: The War May Be Ending. What Did Epic Fury Cost?
gist: CSIS estimates the February 2026 U.S. campaign against Iran (Operation Epic Fury) cost the DOD roughly $40 billion, with munitions ($26.1B) the dominant component and base damage ($4-9.4B) the largest source of disagreement with official estimates. Neither the FY26 nor FY27 budgets include war costs, setting up a contentious funding fight in Congress.

insights.claim: CSIS estimates the total DOD cost of the February 2026 U.S. campaign against Iran (Operation Epic Fury) at roughly $40 billion (range $34B-$42B), covering only incremental costs above the existing budget across six categories.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Establishes a credible nonpartisan baseline for the supplemental appropriation fight that will shape near-term defense spending and reprogramming risk.
insights.locator: With a ceasefire agreement signed, negotiations ongoing

insights.claim: The CSIS $40B estimate sits below the Iran War Cost Tracker's $113B (which extrapolated a $1B/day early figure) and Moody's $132B Americans-cost figure, but exceeds early official DOD estimates of $25B-$29B that excluded base damage.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Wide dispersion across estimates ($25B to $132B) signals high uncertainty; investors tracking defense budgets should anchor to incremental-cost framing rather than headline daily-burn extrapolations.
insights.locator: How This Estimate Compares to Others

insights.claim: Daily war costs were highly non-linear, falling sharply after the first six days ($11.3 billion) as coalition forces gained air superiority and shifted from expensive long-range missiles to cheaper short-range munitions.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The cost curve shows munitions mix, not duration, drove spend; demand surge concentrated in high-end strike missiles rather than across the board.

insights.claim: Munitions are the single largest war cost at $26.1 billion, with U.S. forces firing 13,629 strike munitions against more than 13,000 targets, plus 49 Tomahawks on June 10 during ceasefire skirmishing; all inventories require replacement given prewar stocks were inadequate.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Massive replenishment demand for high-end strike missiles (Tomahawk, JASSM, PrSM/ATACMS) points to multiyear order backlogs for prime missile makers like RTX and Lockheed Martin.

insights.claim: Low-cost strike alternatives such as LUCAS, a U.S. copy of the Iranian Shahed 136 drone, are not yet in serial production, leaving the U.S. reliant on expensive long-range munitions.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A cost-asymmetry gap creates a procurement opening for cheap attritable munitions; watch for accelerated low-cost drone/missile programs and suppliers entering serial production.
insights.locator: Note that all the long-range attack munitions

insights.claim: Cost per equivalent payload varies dramatically: a JDAM-guided dumb bomb delivers a 1,000-lb payload for about $100,000 versus $2.6 million for a Tomahawk, making interceptor and munition mix the decisive cost driver.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Demonstrates the procurement value of cheap precision-guidance kits; expect demand emphasis on JDAM-class upgrades over premium cruise missiles where air superiority allows.
insights.quote: A single dumb bomb fitted with a JDAM guidance kit and a Tomahawk both deliver 1,000 lb payloads with extreme precision but at a much different cost ($2.6 million versus $100,000).
insights.locator: The coalition's success in rapidly suppressing

themes.theme: Base damage, equipment losses and Iranian precision

insights.claim: Base and fixed-infrastructure damage is the most contested cost category at $4.0B-$9.4B (rising to $3.2B-$9.2B if Washington Post's 228-structure count is used), and it is excluded entirely from official DOD estimates because the Pentagon is reviewing its Middle East posture.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The single biggest gap between CSIS and official figures; a satellite-imagery embargo and posture review mean true reconstruction costs and locations remain unresolved budget risk.
insights.locator: Damage to Bases and Fixed Infrastructure

insights.claim: Iranian strikes on U.S. bases were highly precise with no random craters, suggesting Iran developed targeting from decades of preparation and on-the-ground agents, possibly aided by Russian or Chinese intelligence for mobile targets.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Demonstrated adversary precision-strike capability against fixed U.S. bases raises the strategic value of base hardening, dispersal, and active missile defense investment.
insights.quote: The images make clear the Iranian strikes were precise. There are no random craters indicating misses or wasted assets.
insights.locator: The images make clear the Iranian strikes

insights.claim: The Congressional Research Service identified 42 lost or damaged aircraft (mostly uncrewed drones), with replacement costs of $1.8B-$3.5B; the U.S. deliberately risked uncrewed over crewed aircraft.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Attrition concentrated in drones validates the unmanned-systems doctrine and points to replacement demand skewing toward attritable platforms plus high-end fighters (F-15EX, F-35A).

insights.claim: Higher fuel prices added $1.4B in DOD costs as the budgeted $3.67/gallon jet fuel rose to about $4.46/gallon, while the Cost of War Project estimated $40B in additional fuel costs to the broader American public.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Energy-price spillover from Hormuz disruption is the main macro channel; a stable peace and reopened strait should ease fuel inflation over the following six months.

insights.claim: Future veterans' costs are projected at $400 million annually and roughly $12 billion over 30 years for the ~70,000 service members involved, far below Iraq/Afghanistan levels due to shorter duration and fewer personnel.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A large off-budget future liability with no accrual system; signals long-tail federal obligations that fall outside current appropriation debates.

insights.claim: No war costs are funded in the FY2026 DOD budget or the FY2027 proposal, and defense officials warn of an operating-funds shortage by late summer 2026 unless covered via a supplemental, FY2027 amendment, or reprogramming.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Near-term liquidity gap for the Pentagon creates timing risk; watch for a supplemental request, which defense hawks prefer to avoid diverting modernization funds.

insights.claim: The $153 billion in FY2026 reconciliation funds (One Big Beautiful Bill) could theoretically cover war costs, but the legislation provided no transfer authority, so any movement requires new congressional legislation rather than simple reprogramming.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Funding war costs becomes a contentious legislative battle attracting antiwar sentiment, including among Republicans; budget uncertainty persists for defense contractors awaiting replenishment orders.
insights.quote: The shooting war has ended, but the budget war has just begun.
insights.locator: In theory, some of the $153 billion

source_meta.author: Mark F. Cancian and Chris H. Park
